content:
# Acts Chapter 24 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 255-256 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_024_page_001.jpg, ACT_024_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And after five days the high priest Ananias came down with the elders and a certain orator, Tertullus, who made-known to the governor against Paul.

**2** And he having been called, Tertullus began to accuse [him], saying, "Much peace obtaining through you, and reforms coming-about to this nation through your forethought,

**3** both in every way and in every place we receive [it], most excellent Felix, with all thankfulness.

**4** But that I might not further hinder you, I urge you to hear us briefly in your clemency.

**5** For having found this man [to be] a pestilence and moving insurrection among all the Jews throughout the inhabited-earth, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes,

**6** who also attempted to profane the temple; whom also we seized;

**7** [omitted in Sinaiticus]

**8** from whom you yourself having examined [him] will be able concerning all these things to know-fully of what we accuse him."

**9** And the Jews also joined-in-the-attack, asserting these things to be so.

**10** And Paul answered, the governor having nodded to him to speak: "Knowing [you] to be a judge to this nation for many years, cheerfully the [things] concerning myself I make-defense.

**11** You being able to know that not more than twelve days are to me from which I went up to worship in Jerusalem;

**12** and neither in the temple they found me disputing with anyone, or making a gathering of a crowd, neither in the synagogues nor throughout the city;

**13** nor are they able to prove to you the [things] concerning which they now accuse me.

**14** But I confess this to you, that according to the Way which they call a sect, so I serve the ancestral God, believing all the [things] according to the law and the [things] having been written in the prophets,

**15** having hope toward God, which also these [men] themselves await, [that there] is about to be a resurrection of the dead, both of righteous and of unrighteous.

**16** In this also I myself exercise [myself] to have a blameless conscience toward God and men always.

**17** And after many years I came [to] bring alms to my nation and offerings;

**18** in which they found me having been purified in the temple, not with a crowd nor with tumult;

**19** but certain Jews from Asia, who ought before you to be present and to accuse, if they should have anything against me --

**20** or let these [men] themselves say what wrong-doing they found [when] I stood before the Sanhedrin,

**21** or concerning this one voice which I cried out having stood among them, that 'Concerning [the] resurrection of [the] dead I am being judged today before you.'"

**22** And Felix deferred them, having known more accurately the [things] concerning the Way, saying, "When Lysias the commander comes down, I will determine the [things] concerning you."

**23** Having commanded the centurion for him to be kept, and to have relaxation, and to hinder none of his own to minister to him.

**24** And after certain days, Felix having arrived with Drusilla his own wife, being a Jewess, he sent-for Paul and heard him concerning the faith in Christ Jesus.

**25** And he reasoning concerning righteousness and self-control and the judgment, the [one] being about [to come], Felix having become afraid answered, "For the [time] now being, go; and having obtained a season I will summon you."

**26** At the same time also hoping that money will be given to him by Paul; therefore also more frequently sending-for him he was conversing with him.

**27** And two years having been fulfilled, Felix received a successor, Porcius Festus; and Felix, wanting to lay-down a favor to the Jews, left Paul having been bound.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "made-known" (enephanisan):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝΕΦΑΝΙΣΑΝ, meaning "they made a formal presentation" or "laid information before" the governor. This is a technical legal term for filing charges in a Roman judicial proceeding.

2. **Verse 2 -- "reforms" (diorthomaton):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΟΡΘΩΜΑΤΩΝ, meaning "corrections" or "administrative improvements." Tertullus opens with conventional forensic flattery (captatio benevolentiae), praising Felix's governance despite the historical reality that Felix's administration was notoriously corrupt and violent (cf. Tacitus, Annals 12.54).

3. **Verse 5 -- "pestilence" (loimon):** Sinaiticus reads ΛΟΙΜΟΝ, literally "a plague." This is a strong forensic insult -- Tertullus characterizes Paul not merely as a troublemaker but as a disease upon the body politic. The same word is used in the LXX for literal pestilence.

4. **Verse 5 -- "Nazarenes" (Nazoraion):** Sinaiticus reads ΝΑΖΩΡΑΙΩΝ. This is one of the earliest external labels for the Christian movement. The term derives from Nazareth and is used as a sectarian designation. It appears to be the term used by Jewish opponents rather than by Christians themselves.

5. **Verses 6-8 -- The "Western" interpolation:** Sinaiticus omits the longer reading found in later manuscripts (the so-called Western text), which adds at verse 6b: "and according to our law we wanted to judge [him]," verse 7: "But Lysias the commander having come with great force took [him] out of our hands, commanding his accusers to come before you," and begins verse 8 differently. Sinaiticus moves directly from the seizure (v. 6) to the examination (v. 8). This shorter reading is considered more original by most textual critics. I have marked verse 7 as omitted accordingly.

6. **Verse 10 -- "many years":** Felix had governed Judea from approximately 52 CE. Paul's trial likely occurred around 57-58 CE, making Felix's tenure approximately five to six years -- enough for Paul's rhetorical "many years" though not the decades implied by the flattery.

7. **Verse 14 -- "the Way" (ten hodon):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗΝ ΟΔΟΝ. This is the self-designation of early Christianity (cf. Acts 9:2; 19:9, 23; 22:4). Paul reclaims the term from Tertullus's dismissive "sect" (hairesis), insisting that "the Way" represents authentic worship of the ancestral God, not a deviation.

8. **Verse 15 -- "both of righteous and of unrighteous":** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΚΑΙΩΝ ΤΕ ΚΑΙ ΑΔΙΚΩΝ. Paul here affirms a universal resurrection, not merely of the righteous. This double resurrection is a distinctively Pharisaic (and Christian) teaching that Sadducees rejected.

9. **Verse 17 -- "alms" (eleemosynas):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΛΕΗΜΟΣΥΝΑΣ. This is Paul's sole reference in Acts to the collection for the Jerusalem saints, the project that dominated his later epistles (Rom 15:25-28; 1 Cor 16:1-4; 2 Cor 8-9). Luke uses the general term "alms" rather than the theological language Paul employs in his letters.

10. **Verse 19 -- Anacoluthon:** Paul's sentence breaks off grammatically. He begins "but certain Jews from Asia, who ought to be present..." but never completes the thought with a main verb. This grammatical rupture (anacoluthon) may reflect genuine emotional agitation, or Luke's faithful preservation of the oral character of the defense.

11. **Verse 22 -- "deferred" (anebaleto):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΕΒΑΛΕΤΟ, the aorist middle of anaballo, meaning "to put off, postpone, defer." Felix neither acquits nor condemns. His "more accurate knowledge" of the Way may indicate prior familiarity with Christianity through his Jewish wife Drusilla.

12. **Verse 24 -- "Drusilla":** Sinaiticus reads ΔΡΟΥΣΙΛΛΗ. She was the youngest daughter of Herod Agrippa I, making her a great-granddaughter of Herod the Great. Felix had enticed her away from her first husband, King Azizus of Emesa. Her Jewish identity explains Felix's access to information about "the Way."

13. **Verse 25 -- "self-control" (egkrateias):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΓΚΡΑΤΕΙΑΣ. Paul's choice to discourse on "righteousness, self-control, and coming judgment" before Felix and Drusilla -- whose illicit union was well known -- constitutes a pointed personal application of the gospel message. Felix's fear (emphobos genomenos) is a natural response.

14. **Verse 26 -- "hoping that money will be given":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΛΠΙΖΩΝ ΟΤΙ ΧΡΗΜΑΤΑ ΔΟΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ΑΥΤΩ ΥΠΟ ΤΟΥ ΠΑΥΛΟΥ. Luke exposes Felix's corruption: his repeated interviews with Paul were motivated by bribery expectations, not genuine interest in the faith. Such judicial bribery was illegal under the Lex Iulia de repetundis but widely practiced.

15. **Verse 27 -- "two years" (dietias):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΕΤΙΑΣ. Whether this denotes two years of Paul's imprisonment or the remaining duration of Felix's governorship is debated. Felix was recalled by Nero and replaced by Festus around 59-60 CE. The "favor" (charin) Felix sought to deposit with the Jews was leaving Paul in chains -- a political gesture to mitigate Jewish complaints against his administration.

-- EMET
